concept.headline: Julia arrays are 1-indexed, mutable, and typed. push! appends, pop! removes. Access with arr[i]. Mutating functions follow the ! convention.
concept.explanation: Julia arrays (Vector{T}) are created with [] literals. They are 1-indexed — the first element is at index 1. Mutating functions are named with a trailing ! by convention: push!(arr, val) appends, pop!(arr) removes the last element, sort!(arr) sorts in place. Non-mutating versions (sort, reverse) return new arrays. Use length(), sum(), minimum(), maximum() for aggregation.
concept.syntax:
# Create
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]

# Access (1-indexed!)
nums[1]    # 3
nums[end]  # 9 — last element

# Mutate
push!(nums, 2)   # append
pop!(nums)       # remove last

# Aggregate
length(nums)     # count
sum(nums)        # total
sort(nums)       # new sorted array
sort!(nums)      # sort in place
keyPoints: 1-indexed: first element at arr[1], last at arr[end]
push!(arr, v) appends; pop!(arr) removes last
! suffix = mutates in place. Without ! = returns new array
length, sum, minimum, maximum for common aggregations

demo.intro: Watch an array being created, mutated with push!/pop!, and aggregated.
demo.code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
println(nums[1])
println(nums[end])
push!(nums, 9)
println(nums)
pop!(nums)
println(length(nums))
println(sum(nums))

explanation.why: Square bracket literal infers element type as Int64
explanation.intent: Show array literal creation and automatic type inference
explanation.misconception: Julia arrays are typed — Vector{Int64} means every element must be an Int64 (or promotable to Int64). Pushing a String to this array would throw a MethodError.

explanation.why: Julia is 1-indexed — index 1 is the first element
explanation.intent: Show 1-based indexing — the most common Julia gotcha for newcomers
explanation.misconception: Julia uses 1-based indexing, NOT 0-based like Python, JavaScript, or C. nums[0] would throw a BoundsError.

explanation.why: end is a special index that refers to the last element (equivalent to nums[length(nums)])
explanation.intent: Show the end keyword for last-element access
explanation.misconception: end works in any index expression: nums[end-1] is the second-to-last element. This is much cleaner than computing length()-1 manually.

explanation.what: push!(nums, 9) — appends 9 to the end of nums
explanation.why: push! mutates the array in place (hence the !)
explanation.misconception: push! modifies the ORIGINAL array. There is no return value you need to capture — nums itself is updated. This differs from Python's append() which also modifies in place.
Julia arrays print with brackets: [1, 2, 3] — no extra 'Array' prefix

explanation.what: pop!(nums) — removes and returns the last element (9)
explanation.why: pop! removes the last element and returns it
explanation.intent: Show pop! removing the last element
explanation.misconception: pop! returns the removed value — you can capture it: val = pop!(nums). Here we ignore it.

explanation.what: length(nums) → 5 — back to 5 elements after pop!
explanation.why: push! added 1 element, pop! removed 1 — net change: 0
explanation.misconception: length() counts elements, not the last index. Since arrays are 1-indexed, the last valid index equals length(nums).
sum() works on any numeric collection — it does not modify the array

prediction.question:
What is the output?

arr = [10, 20, 30]
push!(arr, 40)
println(arr[end])
prediction.explanation: push!(arr, 40) appends 40 to the array making it [10,20,30,40]. arr[end] accesses the last element, which is 40.

explanation.afterDemo: Julia arrays are the workhorse of numerical computing. The 1-based indexing aligns with mathematics and Fortran — if you come from Python the shift takes a moment but becomes natural. The ! convention makes it immediately clear whether a function mutates its argument or returns a new value — a design discipline that prevents many bugs.
keyTakeaways: 1-indexed: arr[1] is first, arr[end] is last. arr[0] throws BoundsError.
push!(arr, v) appends in place. pop!(arr) removes and returns the last element.
! suffix = mutates in place. sort(arr) → new array; sort!(arr) → modifies arr.

practice.prompt: Start with arr = [5, 2, 8, 1, 9]. Push 3 onto it, sort! it in place, then print the first and last elements on separate lines.
practice.starterCode:
arr = [5, 2, 8, 1, 9]
# push 3
# sort in place
# print first and last
practice.hint: push!(arr, 3) then sort!(arr) in place. arr[1] is first, arr[end] is last.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! After push and sort: [1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 9]. First=1, last=9.

summary.title: Arrays — Complete
takeaways: 1-indexed arrays: arr[1] is first. arr[end] is last.
push!/pop! append/remove at the end. ! means mutates in place.
Aggregate with length(), sum(), minimum(), maximum(). sort() → new; sort!() → in place.
